Title: Proanthocyanidins - a final frontier in flavonoid research?

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal seed-coat proanthocyanidin accumulation, likely through transport into vacuolar compartment
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed specifically in endothelial layer of developing seed coat
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Membrane transporter; implicated in vacuolar transport pathway
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Biochemical function remained unresolved in this review; thought to participate in transport processes required for PA synthesis/assembly
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT12 mutants show transparent testa with reduced proanthocyanidin pigment. 2. TT12 encodes a MATE transporter specifically expressed in seed endothelium. 3. It is implicated in intracellular transport required for PA synthesis or assembly.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Likely essential component for successful PA engineering because monomer transport/storage may limit accumulation.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown biochemical specificity may complicate transfer across species.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proper proanthocyanidin pigment accumulation in seed coat, likely by facilitating flavonoid transport
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; heterologous complementation comparison with petunia AN9
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Petunia AN9 complements anthocyanin transport but not PA accumulation in tt19, implying PA-specific transport requirements; whether TT19 binds flavonoids or forms glutathione conjugates remained unclear
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. tt19 mutants have reduced PA pigment. 2. TT19 encodes a GST family protein involved in transport processes. 3. Anthocyanin transport functions are not sufficient to replace TT19 for PA accumulation.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important accessory gene for reconstructing PA accumulation, especially vacuolar sequestration.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Specificity for PA-related cargo remains unresolved.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Regulatory locus for general flavonoid pathway
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Affects beer quality via haze formation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Regulates general flavonoid pathway impacting barley proanthocyanidin accumulation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ant13 mutants appeared to have reduced agronomic performance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ant13 is a barley regulatory locus affecting the general flavonoid pathway. 2. It influences PA-deficient phenotypes identified among ant mutants. 3. Agronomic penalties were noted for Ant13 mutants.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant for breeding PA-free malting barley, but potential agronomic penalties must be considered.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Affects beer quality via haze formation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Probable regulatory gene affecting biosynthesis of both anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Molecular identity was not defined in the review
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ant21 is probably a regulatory gene in barley. 2. It affects both anthocyanin and PA biosynthesis. 3. It emerged from classical ant mutant analyses.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful genetic locus for barley quality breeding, though molecular cloning is needed for deployment.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Review across multiple plant species; key source gene from Desmodium and pathway discussed in Arabidopsis, barley, legumes, tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies monomer building block / starter unit for proanthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Isoflavone reductase-like member of the RED reductase-epimerase-dehydrogenase supergene family
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of (+)-catechin monomer for proanthocyanidin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assay; gene cloning; correlation of activity with proanthocyanidin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in tannin-rich tissues such as leaves/seeds of legumes; highest activity in young leaves in sanfoin
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Cloned in 2003 from Desmodium uncinatum; Arabidopsis appears not to possess a clear ortholog; genetic validation beyond biochemical evidence was not yet reported in this review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LAR was identified as the enzyme converting leucoanthocyanidin to (+)-catechin. 2. It defines the 2,3-trans flavan-3-ol branch supplying proanthocyanidin monomers. 3. LAR activity correlates with proanthocyanidin accumulation in several legumes and barley, but direct genetic validation remained limited at the time of review.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for engineering proanthocyanidin content and monomer composition in forage legumes and other crops, though additional genes are likely required for stable pathway completion.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating single early/branch enzymes may give inconsistent proanthocyanidin phenotypes due to pathway complexity.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Medicago truncatula; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins such as cyanidin to 2,3-cis flavan-3-ols, primarily (-)-epicatechin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies monomer building block / likely extension-unit precursor for proanthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream branch step after anthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-control step proposed but not conclusively established
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DFR-related reductase; member of an oxidoreductase family
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BAN abolishes seed-coat proanthocyanidins and causes precocious anthocyanin accumulation; ectopic expression promotes proanthocyanidin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Arabidopsis mutant analysis; recombinant enzyme assay; transgenic expression in tobacco; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Primarily young seed coats/endothelium in Medicago; developing Arabidopsis seed coat
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In transgenic tobacco, constitutive expression produced proanthocyanidins in pigmented flower regions; with PAP1 coexpression also enabled leaf accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Likely cytosolic enzyme; exact localization not established in review
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Constitutive expression used in tobacco; specific promoter not stated in excerpt
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Originally proposed to encode LAR but later shown to encode a new enzyme, ANR; AtANR and MtANR differ in cofactor usage and substrate preference; minor formation of alternative stereoisomers in vitro discussed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAN was redefined as anthocyanidin reductase rather than LAR. 2. BAN/ANR converts cyanidin to (-)-epicatechin, revealing anthocyanidins as intermediates in proanthocyanidin biosynthesis. 3. Arabidopsis ban mutants lack proanthocyanidins and accumulate anthocyanins, while Medicago BAN expression in tobacco induces condensed tannins.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering condensed tannin accumulation in forage crops and nutraceutical traits, especially in combination with regulators or substrate-supplying genes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ectopic expression alone can be tissue-limited and dependent on anthocyanin substrate availability; additional factors beyond substrate supply are required.

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: TT18; also called LDOX in some contexts
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana and multiple plant species
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis and multiple plant species discussed in review
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Shared late flavonoid step feeding anthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes leucoanthocyanidin to anthocyanidin, which can then be reduced by ANR to (-)-epicatechin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for proanthocyanidin monomer biosynthesis via ANR
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Late shared pathway step upstream of ANR
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal proanthocyanidin accumulation; ans mutants are deficient in proanthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Correlates with early fruit developmental stages in bilberry and developing seed tissues in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The revised pathway places ANS as part of the proanthocyanidin pathway, not just anthocyanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANS generates anthocyanidin intermediates required for the ANR-dependent epicatechin branch. 2. Genetic evidence from Arabidopsis shows ans mutations reduce proanthocyanidins. 3. The review repositions ANS as a bona fide enzyme of proanthocyanidin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful pathway component for stacked engineering of condensed tannins, though alone insufficient to direct flux specifically to proanthocyanidins.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux competition with anthocyanin glucosylation may complicate engineering outcomes.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Multiple plant species; discussed prominently in Medicago truncatula, barley, Lotus corniculatus
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Multiple plant species including Medicago, barley, Arabidopsis, Lotus corniculatus
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late shared flavonoid pathway supplying leucoanthocyanidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols to leucoanthocyanidins, precursors for catechin and anthocyanidin/epicatechin formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for proanthocyanidin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Mid-to-late upstream step before LAR/ANS/ANR branch outcomes
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; possible specific isoform/complex involvement discussed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects proanthocyanidin accumulation, but engineering/downregulation gives variable outcomes depending on species and context
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical studies; mutant analysis in barley; antisense and overexpression in Lotus corniculatus hairy roots and plants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High in young leaves in tannin-rich legumes; inducible by herbivory, wounding and methyl jasmonate in Populus tremuloides
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Two DFR genes present in Medicago truncatula with differing substrate preferences; single gene in some species; may operate in enzyme complexes affecting channeling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR supplies leucoanthocyanidin precursors for both catechin and epicatechin branches of proanthocyanidin biosynthesis. 2. Genetic and transgenic manipulation of DFR affects proanthocyanidins but often inconsistently. 3. Specific isoforms or complexes may help determine flux toward proanthocyanidins.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for pathway engineering, but single-gene manipulation is unreliable and should likely be combined with downstream branch genes/regulators.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Antisense or overexpression can cause variable or paradoxical effects on proanthocyanidin levels and composition.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Multiple plant species; identified genetically in barley mutants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Barley and multiple plants discussed in review
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates flavanones to dihydroflavonols, supplying precursors for late flavonoid/proanthocyanidin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proanthocyanidin production in barley genetic studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as one of the structural loci identified from barley ant mutants affecting proanthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3H is an upstream structural gene necessary to supply dihydroflavonol precursors. 2. Barley ant17 mutants identified F3H as required for proanthocyanidin accumulation. 3. It is not specific to the proanthocyanidin branch but is essential to pathway flux.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful background pathway target but unlikely sufficient alone for selective proanthocyanidin engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation likely affects multiple flavonoid end products beyond proanthocyanidins.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Multiple plant species; identified genetically in barley mutants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Barley and Lotus corniculatus studies discussed in review
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense, forage quality trait, nutraceutical/health-promoting polyphenol
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chalcone to flavanone, establishing the stereochemical framework of downstream flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required upstream for proanthocyanidin accumulation; antisense downregulation in Lotus gave paradoxical effects in some contexts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Barley ant30 mutant genetics; antisense studies in Lotus corniculatus hairy roots
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Early pathway manipulation produced genotype-dependent and sometimes paradoxical proanthocyanidin responses
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHI is an early flavonoid enzyme required to establish pathway flux toward proanthocyanidins. 2. Barley ant30 genetics linked CHI deficiency to proanthocyanidin loss. 3. Early-pathway engineering can produce inconsistent condensed tannin outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Broad flux-control target, but unsuitable alone for precise proanthocyanidin engineering due to pleiotropy.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely alters anthocyanins and other flavonoids broadly; can produce paradoxical responses.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates/upregulates late flavonoid and proanthocyanidin-specific genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as a master positive regulator required for seed-coat proanthocyanidin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master regulator of the proanthocyanidin branch in Arabidopsis seed coat
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; ectopic expression; promoter analysis
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions with TT8 and TTG1 regulatory complex
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT2 positively regulates multiple late flavonoid and proanthocyanidin genes. 2. tt2 mutants lack BAN transcript and proanthocyanidin deposition. 3. Ectopic TT2 can induce BAN and TT8 and upregulate DFR and LDOX.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising regulator for coordinated activation of the proanthocyanidin pathway, likely more effective than manipulating individual enzymes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely requires appropriate partner factors and tissue context for full function.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Deletion of BAN promoter elements associated with TT2/TT8 binding abolishes endothelium-specific BAN expression.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates BAN and DFR in concert with TT2/TTG1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for BAN expression and proanthocyanidin deposition in seeds
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential positive component of the TT2-TT8-TTG1 complex controlling PA biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT8 is a bHLH regulator necessary for BAN and DFR expression. 2. tt8 mutants lack proanthocyanidin deposition. 3. TT8 acts with TT2 and TTG1 in the seed-coat regulatory circuit.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful in regulator stacking to activate condensed tannin biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Single-factor expression may be insufficient without MYB/WD40 partners.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of the late flavonoid regulator class in Arabidopsis.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Proanthocyanidin biosynthesis and seed coat developmental context
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positively required for activation of PA/anthocyanin regulatory network
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proanthocyanidin and anthocyanin biosynthesis; loss disrupts seed proanthocyanidins
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential scaffold-like component enabling MYB-bHLH regulatory complexes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; yeast two-hybrid interaction studies
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: PAP1; PAP2; GL3; EGL3; TT2; TT8
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TTG1 is required for both anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin biosynthesis. 2. It physically interacts with MYB and bHLH factors. 3. ttg1 loss abolishes BAN expression and seed PA deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important enabling regulator for reconstituting full proanthocyanidin regulatory modules.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Also affects trichome differentiation, root hair formation and mucilage production, implying pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ectopic PAP1 does not induce anthocyanin in ttg1 background, highlighting TTG1 indispensability.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Necessary for correct cell morphology and proanthocyanidin biosynthesis in seed body
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Controls endothelial cell development and BAN expression pattern needed for PA deposition
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT1 is required for proper endothelium development and PA accumulation. 2. It affects the spatial pattern of BAN expression. 3. Its effect is specific to seed body rather than chalaza/micropyle.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May help establish correct tissue context for PA engineering in seeds.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Developmental regulator, so manipulation may alter seed coat morphology.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Placed in the developmental regulator class rather than the core MYB-bHLH-WD40 module.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Developmental control of seed endothelium pigmentation and PA deposition
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for development and pigmentation of seed endothelium supporting proanthocyanidin deposition
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Provides developmental competence for PA accumulation in seed body
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; ectopic TT2 expression rescue observations
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TT16 is needed for proper seed endothelium development and PA deposition. 2. It affects BAN expression pattern. 3. Ectopic TT2 can overcome PA deposition defects in tt16, indicating TT16 acts partly through developmental control.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potentially useful for seed-specific engineering where tissue differentiation limits PA accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may affect seed development and pigmentation broadly.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Proanthocyanidin biosynthesis with seed coat developmental processes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive modulation of PA regulatory genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Modulates proanthocyanidin biosynthesis and requires TTG1 for function
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important developmental/regulatory modulator of PA accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Seed coat; also linked to trichome and mucilage traits
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TTG2 modulates PA biosynthesis along with trichome differentiation and mucilage production. 2. It regulates BAN and TT2. 3. TTG1 function is required for TTG2-mediated PA control.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could help tune PA deposition in specific seed tissues, though pleiotropy is expected.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Also affects trichomes and mucilage production.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Placed in developmental regulatory layer of Arabidopsis PA control.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin pathway activation supplying substrate for proanthocyanidin engineering
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases anthocyanin and, when combined with BAN, facilitates epicatechin and proanthocyanidin accumulation in tobacco leaves
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Indirect but useful activator of substrate supply for engineered PA production
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive overexpression causes high-level anthocyanin production
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with TTG1 in yeast two-hybrid; likely part of MYB-bHLH-WD40 complexes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP1 is a strong activator of anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. Alone it mainly enhances anthocyanins, but in combination with Medicago BAN it enables leaf epicatechin and PA accumulation in tobacco. 3. It illustrates the importance of substrate supply for engineering the PA pathway.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful partner in transgene stacks with ANR/BAN and possibly other PA genes to enable substrate-rich tissues.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong anthocyanin pigmentation and broad flavonoid rewiring may accompany use.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper treats PAP1 mainly as an anthocyanin regulator, but it is experimentally central in PA engineering context.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin/flavonoid biosynthesis upstream of PA engineering
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Constitutive anthocyanin production; relevance to PA control is indirect through shared pathway activation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Indirect regulator of pathway flux rather than a PA-specific master regulator
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP2 is an Arabidopsis C1 ortholog activating anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. It demonstrates multiplicity of flavonoid regulators in Arabidopsis. 3. Its role in PA accumulation in this review is supportive rather than direct.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible auxiliary regulator for boosting precursor flux.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad anthocyanin activation and pleiotropic pigmentation effects.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review summarizes it among transcription factors relevant to flavonoid/proanthocyanidin network.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin/flavonoid biosynthesis in shared regulatory network
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates anthocyanin biosynthesis with MYB/WD40 partners
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports flavonoid pathway activation; relevance to PA pathway is as part of broader regulatory network
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally relevant shared regulator, not specifically established as PA master regulator in the review
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression activation in petals; interaction studies referenced
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GL3 is an Arabidopsis R ortholog. 2. It can activate anthocyanin biosynthesis in heterologous petals. 3. It contributes to the wider flavonoid regulatory landscape surrounding PA synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary factor in multigene regulatory engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: More directly linked to anthocyanins than PA-specific deposition.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Shared flavonoid regulatory factor with indirect relevance to PA control
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression activation in petals; interaction studies referenced
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EGL3 is an Arabidopsis bHLH flavonoid regulator. 2. It can activate anthocyanin formation in heterologous petals. 3. It is part of the regulatory network relevant to PA pathway context.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential supporting regulator in engineering stacks.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic due to broader epidermal developmental roles.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not described as a direct PA-specific regulator in the review.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Homeodomain protein of the HD-GLABRA2 group
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin-related regulation in shared late flavonoid pathway
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indirectly influences pathway flux through DFR
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Minor/indirect regulator relative to TT2-TT8-TTG1 core
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANL2 affects anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. It regulates DFR expression. 3. Its relevance to PA biosynthesis is indirect via shared pathway genes.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct value for PA-specific engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in review's summary table of TFs related to PA biosynthesis context.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Developmental control of seed pathway boundaries impacting PA accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes upstream developmental control over PA regulatory network
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FUS3 is an upstream embryo developmental regulator. 2. It controls TTG1 expression. 3. It links embryogenesis programs to PA regulatory competence.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potentially relevant for seed-specific PA traits, but likely highly pleiotropic.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed with LEC genes as possible coordinators of PA gene expression after fertilization.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Activation of flavonoid/proanthocyanidin biosynthesis in engineered forage legume
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates flavonoid pathway leading to anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin accumulation under stress
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enabled accumulation of proanthocyanidin in transgenic alfalfa leaves, unlike maize B-Peru or C1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Demonstrated practical ability to induce PA accumulation in a forage crop lacking leaf tannins
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in alfalfa with metabolite phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive transgene; PA phenotype visible under high light or cold stress
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Maize Lc was effective in modulating the pathway in alfalfa, unlike B-Peru or C1. 2. Transgenic alfalfa accumulated anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin in leaves. 3. Stress such as high light or cold was required for visible phenotype.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising engineering regulator for introducing beneficial low-level condensed tannins into forage legumes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Stress dependence and possible pleiotropic pigmentation changes may limit deployment.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Important cross-species regulator example in the review.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light intensity or cold required for phenotype

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots primarily; leaves showed unaffected or suppressed levels
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: DFR; LAR activity changes mirrored phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Constitutive expression altered DFR and LAR activities in a tissue-dependent manner
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased root proanthocyanidin accumulation but had neutral or suppressive effects in leaves
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent/tissue-specific regulator of engineered PA accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive transgenic expression with metabolite and enzyme activity assays
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Constitutive Sn expression increased root PAs in Lotus corniculatus. 2. Leaf PA levels were unaffected or suppressed. 3. DFR and LAR activities paralleled the leaf phenotypes, revealing strong tissue dependence.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulator for root-targeted PA engineering, but unreliable for consistent whole-plant forage tannin enhancement.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Illustrates that regulatory engineering of PAs can produce organ-specific responses.
